skill.name: infsh-cli
skill.description: Run inference.sh cloud AI apps from the belt CLI for image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and Twitter/X automation workflows.
skill.summary: Use the belt CLI to discover and run inference.sh AI apps from an agent workflow.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: The highest-risk items are confirmed pipe-to-shell installer instructions and a manual install flow that evaluates remote manifest data in shell commands. Most Markdown links, placeholder API key references, shell completion examples, and read-only command table entries are false positives, but the skill still has meaningful risk from cloud execution, local file uploads, and social media automation.

remediation.issue: Remote installer is piped directly to sh.
remediation.suggestion: Replace curl-to-sh instructions with download, inspect, checksum, and signature verification steps before execution.

remediation.issue: Manual install uses shell command substitution with remote manifest data.
remediation.suggestion: Use a fixed release URL or a small verified installer that parses manifests safely without shell evaluation.

remediation.issue: Examples upload local files to cloud services.
remediation.suggestion: Require explicit user confirmation before passing local paths, and warn users to avoid sensitive files.

remediation.issue: Social media automation can post or act on external accounts.
remediation.suggestion: Require explicit confirmation for posting, direct messages, following, liking, and reposting actions.

static_findings.snippet: | "invalid input" | Schema mismatch | Check `belt app get` for required fields |
> **Install the belt CLI skill:** `npx skills add belt-sh/cli`
> **What does the installer do?** The [install script](https://cli.inference.sh) detects your OS and
| Browse the app store | `belt app store` |
| Search the store | `belt app store search "flux"` |
| Filter by category | `belt app store --category image` |
| Get app details | `belt app get google/veo-3-1-fast` |
| Generate sample input | `belt app sample google/veo-3-1-fast --save input.json` |
| Run app | `belt app run google/veo-3-1-fast --input input.json` |
| Run without waiting | `belt app run <app> --input input.json --no-wait` |

finding_verdicts.reason: The URL is used in a curl command that downloads a remote installer for immediate shell execution. The network dependency is intentional and executable, so it is a real install-time risk.
The URL is used again as the reinstall path and feeds a remote script to the shell. This is an intentional external installer dependency.
This is a placeholder example showing users how to set INFSH_API_KEY. No real secret is embedded and the file does not read or transmit the variable itself.
This troubleshooting row only tells users to check their API key or log in again. It does not contain a credential or code that accesses one.
The documented install command pipes a remote script directly into sh. Users cannot inspect the downloaded script before execution, which is a critical supply-chain risk.
The reinstall command repeats the same curl-to-sh pattern. Executing remote code from a URL without prior review is a critical install-time risk.
The URL appears inside the CLI installation command and supplies a remote script for execution. This is a real external network dependency in the setup path.
The line writes a fish shell completion file to the standard user completion directory. It is a normal documented shell setup action, not hidden persistence or unauthorized file access.
The path is a conventional fish shell completions location under the user config directory. The command is explicit and limited to shell completion installation.
This table documents the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable. It does not include a real secret or code that extracts credentials.
The installation reference pipes a remote script directly into sh. That pattern executes network-delivered code without prior inspection.
This is an example output URL showing where generated files may be hosted. It is not a command, callback, or hidden network operation.
The line documents that users may pass relative file paths, including parent directories, as explicit inputs. It is not code performing path traversal or bypassing access controls.
The line suggests checking an app schema with belt app get after invalid input. This is normal CLI troubleshooting, not host reconnaissance.
The inline command installs an additional CLI skill through npx. Installing tools from a package runner is a real external command and supply-chain risk.
The referenced command block contains curl piped to sh and belt login. It instructs shell execution and authentication setup, so the external command risk is real.

This finding points at the end of the install block, but the surrounding range includes setup commands and installer explanation. The shell execution context is real, though the exact line is weak evidence.
The line is prose explaining what the installer claims to do. It is not itself a shell command or code execution primitive.
The manual install block contains curl downloads, checksum verification, archive extraction, and moving a binary into PATH. These are real external shell commands.
The finding begins at a code fence boundary, but the surrounding section transitions into executable belt examples. The evidence is indirect but still tied to shell command instructions.
The quick examples run multiple belt app commands against cloud services. These are intentional external CLI executions with network effects.
The range introduces local file upload examples that are executed through belt app run. The exact line is a code fence boundary, but the surrounding context is executable CLI usage.
The command block runs belt app commands with local file paths that the CLI uploads. This is real external command execution with possible local data exposure.
The range moves from local upload examples into a command table. It documents executable belt commands, although this specific line is weak evidence.
This table row documents a read-only app store browsing command. The Markdown backticks are not Ruby execution and the command does not alter local state.
This is a documented search command in inline Markdown code. It is not dynamic shell execution or an injection sink.
This row documents filtering app store results. It is a benign CLI reference entry, not a dangerous execution pattern.
The row lists a command that displays the user app list. It may reveal account data to the user, but the static Ruby backtick pattern is not present.
The command retrieves public app details for a named app. It is documentation in Markdown backticks, not shell backtick execution.
The command generates sample input and can save it to input.json. This is a real external CLI command with a local file write side effect.
The command runs a cloud app using an input file. That is intentional external command execution with network processing of user-supplied data.
The command submits a cloud app task without waiting. It can start remote work and consume account resources, so the command risk is real.
The row checks task status for a task ID. It is a documented read operation and the Ruby backtick execution pattern is not actually present.
The related skills block contains multiple npx skills add commands. Installing additional skills from external sources is a real supply-chain action.
The manual install command uses shell command substitution around a remote manifest fetch to select a download URL. This combines network-controlled data with shell evaluation.
The manual install block includes command substitution and file installation steps. Even though it is documentation, it instructs a risky shell workflow.
This is the project homepage link in the Markdown heading. It does not trigger a network request by itself beyond normal documentation rendering.
This is a Markdown image URL used for documentation content. It is not an executable command or data exfiltration endpoint.
The URL is used in a curl-to-sh installer command. It is an executable network dependency and is also covered by the pipe-to-shell finding.
This is a prose link to the install script while explaining installer behavior. The executable risk is captured on the actual command line.
The manual install instructions download checksum data from a fixed external host. This is an intentional network dependency in the install workflow.
The command downloads a remote manifest and uses it to choose a binary URL. That is a real network-dependent installer step.
This is a documentation link to an inference.sh blog page. It is not part of an executable workflow.
This is a documentation link for getting started material. It does not perform a hidden network action.
This is a platform overview documentation link. It is safe reference content, not a command endpoint.
This is a documentation link about the app ecosystem. It is not used for execution or credential handling.
This is a documentation link for CLI setup. The actual installer command is adjudicated separately.
This is a blog link about workflows and agents. It does not create security impact by itself.
This is a blog link about runtimes. It is reference material, not a network call made by the skill.
The manual install command moves a downloaded binary into ~/.local/bin. That modifies the user executable path and can persist a third-party CLI in the account.
The command writes under a hidden home directory path as part of installation. It is explicit, but still a real filesystem modification to a command location.
The primary install instruction pipes a remote script directly into sh. This is a critical remote-code execution supply-chain pattern for marketplace publication.

semantic_findings.title: Local File Upload To Cloud Services
semantic_findings.description: The skill explains that local file paths are automatically uploaded when passed to belt app run. An agent could send sensitive local images, audio, video, or screenshots to cloud services if prompts are not constrained.
semantic_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly states that local file paths are uploaded and gives multiple examples with local media paths. This is an intentional capability with clear data exposure risk.

semantic_findings.title: External Social Media Automation
semantic_findings.description: The skill includes commands for posting to Twitter/X and lists Twitter/X actions such as posting, direct messages, following, liking, and reposting. This can create spam, impersonation, or unauthorized account-action risk if used without explicit user approval.
semantic_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents social posting commands and a category of Twitter/X automation actions. The abuse risk is contextual rather than a single static code pattern.

content.user_title: Run inference.sh AI Apps From the CLI
content.value_statement: Teams need one workflow for many hosted AI models and media tools. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use the belt CLI to discover apps, prepare inputs, run jobs, and track results.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and authenticate the belt CLI for inference.sh.
Runs hosted AI apps with inline JSON or input files.
Finds public apps by category, search query, featured status, or newest listing.
Generates sample input files and checks app schemas before execution.
Tracks long-running tasks and saves results from completed jobs.
Uploads local media files when an app input accepts a file path.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Cloud app usage may consume account credits or quota.
Local file paths can upload user files to external services.
Model availability and app schemas depend on the inference.sh platform.

use_cases.title: Prototype multimodal AI workflows
use_cases.description: Run image, video, audio, search, and LLM apps from one command workflow during product experiments.

use_cases.title: Generate media assets from prompts
use_cases.description: Create images, videos, avatars, 3D assets, or upscaled media using hosted apps without local GPUs.

use_cases.title: Automate repeatable app runs
use_cases.description: Create sample inputs, run jobs without waiting, and poll task results for scripted cloud AI workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Find an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Find inference.sh apps for image generation. Show options by category and explain which one fits a simple product mockup.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic job
prompt_templates.prompt: Use belt to run a text or image app with inline input. Confirm the app name and input before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an input file
prompt_templates.prompt: Get the app schema, create a sample input file, explain the fields I should edit, then run the app after I approve.

prompt_templates.title: Manage an async workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Submit a long-running video job without waiting, capture the task ID, poll status, and save the final result when complete.

output_examples.input: Search for a fast video generation app and prepare a sample input.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists matching video apps, selects a fast Veo option, creates a sample input file, and asks for approval before running.

output_examples.input: Run an image upscale job for a local photo.
output_examples.output: The assistant warns that the local file will be uploaded, confirms the path and app name, then reports the resulting download link.

output_examples.input: Check the status of my previous generation task.
output_examples.output: The assistant requests the task ID if needed, checks the job status, and summarizes whether the result is ready.

best_practices: Confirm app names, inputs, costs, and account actions before running cloud jobs.
Review local file paths before upload and avoid sensitive or private media.
Use generated sample inputs to match each app schema before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers into a shell without inspection and verification.
Do not pass broad directories, secrets, or private files as app inputs.
Do not post to social platforms or send messages without explicit user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help an agent do?
faq.answer: It helps an agent use the belt CLI to discover, run, and track inference.sh cloud AI apps.

faq.question: Does it require an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI must be installed and authenticated before most app runs will work.

faq.question: Can it use local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI can upload local files when an app input accepts a file path.

faq.question: Can it run long jobs?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents no-wait task submission and later task status checks.

faq.question: What models or apps are referenced?
faq.answer: The skill references image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and Twitter/X apps.

faq.question: What should users review before use?
faq.answer: Users should review install commands, local file uploads, account costs, and social media actions.
